data.title: Cognyte projects 12% FY26 revenue growth with $43M adjusted EBITDA target
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cognyte Software Ltd. (CGNT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Elad Sharon reported double-digit revenue growth for Q4 2025, with revenue increasing by 13% year-over-year to $94.5 million. Adjusted EBITDA grew 114% year-over-year, reaching $9.3 million. The company generated $19 million in cash flow from operations during the quarter.
  • The company secured major deals in Q4, including a $10 million technology upgrade and multi-year support agreement with a law enforcement agency in EMEA, and five additional deals valued at $5 million or more across various regions.
  • For fiscal year 2025, revenue grew 12% year-over-year to $351 million, with adjusted EBITDA tripling to $29 million. Non-GAAP gross profit rose by 15% year-over-year, reaching $249 million.
  • CFO David Abadi highlighted the company's strong cash position of $113.3 million and no debt. Deferred revenue at the end of Q4 stood at $130.3 million, up from $123.1 million the previous year.
  • The company initiated its share repurchase program during Q4, buying back 586,000 ordinary shares for $5.3 million out of the approved $20 million program.

Outlook

  • For fiscal 2026, Cognyte projects revenue of approximately $392 million, plus or minus 2%, representing around 12% year-over-year growth at the midpoint. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to reach $43 million, a 45% increase year-over-year.
  • Management anticipates total software revenue will make up approximately 87% of total revenue, with professional services contributing the remaining 13%.
  • Non-GAAP gross margin for fiscal 2026 is expected to improve to approximately 71.5%, driven by a better revenue mix and cost structure.
  • Q1 2026 revenue is expected to align with Q4 2025 levels, with sequential growth in subsequent quarters.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 software revenue increased by $9.6 million year-over-year to $83.3 million, representing 88% of total revenue. Recurring revenue reached $47.3 million, or 50% of Q4 revenue, driven by support contracts and subscription offerings.
  • Non-GAAP gross margin for the quarter was 71.5%, with software gross margin improving to 78.9% year-over-year.
  • Professional services revenue grew by $1.2 million year-over-year to $11.2 million, with its gross margin improving significantly to 16.3%.
  • Total RPO at the end of Q4 was $545.8 million, supported by a $20 million annual value contract renewal for three years.

Q&A

  • Mike Cikos, Needham: Asked about U.S. demand trends and potential impacts of policy uncertainties. CEO Sharon stated that U.S. sales cycles are longer due to penetration efforts but noted positive engagement with federal agencies. Sharon emphasized the company's focus on expanding its U.S. market presence and investments in hiring, POCs, and participation in industry conferences.
  • Peter Levine, Evercore: Inquired about demand drivers in the U.S. and globally. Sharon highlighted strong global demand driven by increasing data complexity and advanced analytics needs. The U.S. market was noted for its emphasis on border control and organized crime challenges.
  • Shaw Yale, TD Cowen: Asked about market dynamics outside the U.S. Sharon described robust global demand, particularly for advanced tools to handle growing data volumes and adversarial sophistication, including the use of AI.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed interest in the U.S. market potential, raising questions about demand stability and sales cycle length. The tone was generally inquisitive but optimistic about the company’s growth prospects.
  • Management displayed confidence in fiscal 2026 projections, emphasizing positive engagement with customers and a steady expansion strategy.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the sentiment remained positive, with management maintaining a consistent focus on execution and growth initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue grew sequentially from Q3 2025 to Q4 2025, with Q4 revenue reaching $94.5 million compared to $89 million in Q3. Adjusted EBITDA also increased significantly quarter-over-quarter.
  • Fiscal 2026 guidance reflects continued double-digit growth expectations, consistent with trends observed in previous quarters.
  • Management tone remained consistent, with an emphasis on expanding U.S. market penetration and leveraging growth opportunities globally.
  • Analysts focused more heavily on U.S. demand trends this quarter, reflecting increased interest in specific regional growth strategies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged longer sales cycles in the U.S., particularly with federal agencies, due to rigorous testing and validation processes.
  • Analysts raised concerns about potential impacts of policy uncertainties and attrition on U.S. market penetration.
  • Management outlined strategies to mitigate these challenges, including investments in local sales teams, partnerships, and marketing efforts.

Final Takeaway

Cognyte Software Ltd. delivered strong Q4 2025 results with robust revenue growth, profitability improvements, and significant customer wins. Looking ahead, the company is projecting 12% revenue growth and a 45% increase in adjusted EBITDA for fiscal 2026, supported by its expanding U.S. presence and global demand for advanced analytics solutions. Management remains confident in its strategy to drive sustained growth and operational efficiency.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Arthur J. Gallagher buys Bircroft Insurance Services
Stock index futures tumble as Trump's tariffs stoke global trade war concerns
data.description:
Stock index futures tumbled on Thursday, as President Donald Trump's reciprocal tariffs ignited fears of a potential global trade war. 

S&P 500 futures (SPX) -2.9%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) -2.9%, and Dow futures (INDU) -2.2%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 4 basis points to 4.08%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 4 basis points to 3.82%.

Donald Trump detailed new tariffs that the U.S. is enacting on imports. The minimum baseline tariff will be 10%, and the highest rate for the latest round of tariffs is 49%.

"In short, the tariffs put in place last night were extraordinary both in terms of scale and in how they were calculated, with President Trump announcing reciprocal tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) as he declared a national emergency over the trade deficit," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said. 

Markets have seen a strong risk-off reaction to the tariff announcement, with S&P futures down, which would bring the index back into correction territory if it materializes in the regular session today, Reid added. 

"Trump has tended to retreat rapidly from tariffs that visibly hurt U.S. consumers. Having a 10% near-universal tariff questions how far retreats go. If there is no retreat, markets will price a US recession. If there is a retreat, markets will assume U.S. growth will weaken," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

The direct effect of such taxes raises U.S. price levels by 1.7% to 2.2%—the change in effective tariff rates multiplied by the share of imports. Second-round effects matter. Will U.S. manufacturers raise prices? Will U.S. retailers indulge in profit-led inflation? A 10% tariff increase means around a 4% consumer price increase, but retailers may use the narrative to raise prices more, Donovan added. 

Wall Street's major averages had, however, finished higher on Wednesday, ahead of Trump's announcement of the plan to impose reciprocal tariffs on imports coming into the U.S.

On the economic calendar for Thursday, the March Challenger jobs-cut report and the initial jobless claims reports were due. The latter is expected to rise slightly to 225K.

The March ISM services index and the March PMI composite final will also land today. The former is expected to come in at 53, and the latter is expected at 53.5.   

The Fed balance sheet will come in later in the day.

data.title: Trump tariffs elicit reactions from Canada, Mexico, Taiwan
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump’s announcement of sweeping tariffs across multiple countries has garnered strong criticism worldwide.

Trump has set a base tariff of 10% on all imports into the country, effective from April 5, along with country-specific reciprocal tariffs from April 9.

Mexico and Canada remained absent from Trump’s list of newly tariffed countries on Wednesday, but the 25% tariffs announced earlier this year that came into effect on April 2.

Canada faces 25% levies on steel, aluminum and automobiles, and Prime Minister Mark Carney has said that he plans to “fight these tariffs with countermeasures.” He will be meeting with cabinet ministers and Canada’s premiers to discuss next steps, which are expected to be unveiled on Thursday.

The country has already placed 25% countertariffs against certain US goods such as spirits and wine, and has said it will keep the taxes in place until the U.S. lifts its own measures.

Flavio Volpe, president of Canada’s Automotive Parts Manufacturers’ Association, said on social media the exemption for Canada on Trump’s latest round of tariffs was “like dodging a bullet into the path of a tank.”

Mexico’s President Claudia Sheinbaum, meanwhile, has been clear that the country will not issue countertariffs, but a “comprehensive program” will be released on Thursday.

Taiwan, a key trade partner in high-value semiconductors and advanced tech, has been hit with a 32% reciprocal tariff, which is expected to have a significant impact on its export-reliant economy.

The country’s cabinet called the levies “very unreasonable” and plans to hold discussions with the U.S. on it. President Lai Ching-te has previously vowed to protect the interest of Taiwanese companies.

Potential responses to the tariffs have included reducing its own tariffs and raising energy imports, according to media reports.

Canada ETFs: (EWC), (FXC), (FLCA), (BBCA)

Mexico ETFs: (FLMX), (EWW), (MEXX).

Taiwan-linked tech stocks to watch: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) -3.8% premarket, Micron Technology (MU) -4.2%, ASE Technology (ASX) -2.6%, United Microelectronics (UMC) -1.7%, MediaTek (OTCPK:MDTTF), AUO (OTCPK:AUOTY), Apple supplier Foxconn (OTCPK:FXCOF), known as Hon Hai Precision Industry (OTCPK:HNHAF). 

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Gucci-owner Kering buys stakes in Italian eyewear makers
Apple falls after Trump's 'Liberation Day' tariffs hit key countries
data.description:
Shares of Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) tumbled about 7% premarket on Thursday after President Donald Trump's 'Liberation Day' tariffs were announced on trading partners including China, where the iPhone maker has a large manufacturing presence.

Trump's tariff's will impact key nations in the semiconductor supply chain, including 34% on China, 32% on Taiwan and 24% on Japan.

"Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL), which has built a lot in China, is going to spend $500B to build plants here," Trump said on Wednesday while announcing the tariff plans.

Apple did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Besides manufacturing in China, Apple also depends upon Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) for supplying advanced chips.

India — where Apple is bolstering its manufacturing base as it seeks to diversify beyond China — was hit with a 26% tariff. Apple's Taiwan-based manufacturing partner Foxconn Technology (OTCPK:FXCOF), reportedly, plans to make about 25M to 30M iPhones at its facilities in India this year, over double the number it made last year.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: TSLA, NKE, and more
data.description:
Stock futures slipped Thursday morning as investors weighed President Donald Trump’s broad import tariffs on goods entering the U.S. The revised tariff framework establishes a minimum baseline of 10%, with the highest rate soaring to 49%.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Diageo (NYSE:DEO) +4% - Shares climbed as analysts viewed the U.S. tariff announcement as avoiding the worst-case scenario for the spirits' industry. Goodbody analysts noted that no additional duties were imposed on imports from Canada and Mexico, making the outcome "less bad than feared" for Diageo. Jefferies analyst Edward Mundy highlighted that a potential 200% tariff on EU alcohol imports was averted, offering relief to European distillers.

Biggest stock losers

  • Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) -8% - Shares plunged after reporting its worst quarterly deliveries since 2022, falling significantly short of expectations. Tesla delivered just 336K vehicles in Q1, well below the consensus estimate of 377K, as production disruptions from the Model Y refresh and potential backlash against Elon Musk’s political involvement weighed on results. Wedbush analyst Dan Ives called the numbers a "disaster" and urged Musk to refocus on Tesla rather than political controversies and his ties to Dogecoin. Meanwhile, Politico reported that U.S. President Donald Trump has told insiders that Musk will step back from his role as a governing partner in the coming weeks.
  • Wayfair (NYSE:W) -14%, Nike (NYSE:NKE) -8%, American Eagle (NYSE:AEO) -5% - Shares of major retailers tumbled as the market reacted to the impending 46% tariffs on Vietnam announced by U.S. President Donald Trump. The steep tariffs, set to take effect on April 9, could significantly impact companies in the apparel, furniture, and toy industries, potentially driving up costs. Analysts warn that some corporations may pass these cost increases onto consumers through price hikes, adding to inflationary pressures in the retail sector.

data.title: Novo Nordisk's head of commercial strategy Camilla Sylvest steps down
data.description:
Novo Nordisk (NVO) said Thursday Camilla Sylvest has decided to leave the company, after a distinguished career of 28 years with the drugmaker, seven years as executive vice president of commercial strategy & corporate affairs.

Consequently, as of April 3, Ludovic Helfgott, executive vice president, rare disease, will assume the responsibility for product and portfolio strategy, including commercial strategy, medical affairs and business development across all therapy areas.

Ludovic joined Novo Nordisk in 2019 as executive vice president and has led the establishment of a strategic rare disease portfolio.

Before joining Novo Nordisk (NVO), Ludovic was responsible for the cardiovascular, metabolic and renal portfolios at AstraZeneca.

data.title: Volkswagen mulls price hike in U.S. as tariffs take effect
data.description:
Volkswagen AG (OTCPK:VWAGY) (OTCPK:VLKAF) reportedly plans to add import fees to the sticker prices of its vehicles shipped into the United States, a move that signals Donald Trump’s 25% auto tariffs will have an immediate effect on Europe’s biggest carmaker.

VW sent U.S. dealers a memo informing them of the fees, as well as moves to temporarily halt rail shipments of vehicles from Mexico and hold at port cars shipped from Europe, Automotive News reported. 

Beginning April 2, a 25% tariff will be imposed on all cars not produced in the U.S. The tariff will be phased in, starting at 2.5% and gradually escalating to 25%, marking a significant development for the automotive industry.

Among the German automakers, Porsche and Volkswagen's Audi - two brands with no U.S. production - have both said recently they would assess the option of price increases to mitigate tariff risks.

Automotive ETFs: (NASDAQ:CARZ), (CARU), and (CARD).
